opening: Not production-ready. Every primary control boundary — authentication, query construction, server-side code execution, secret management, and output encoding — is broken or absent. An anonymous internet attacker can achieve administrative access, read the full user database, or execute arbitrary code on the server without chaining multiple vulnerabilities; each capability is independently available through a single entry point.
bullets_intro: Worst-case scenarios behind this verdict — what an attacker could do today:

bullets.title: Full admin takeover using the public repository
bullets.body: The RSA key used to sign every login session is committed to the source code repository; any reader can mint a session token with administrator privileges and access every protected admin route without supplying a valid credential.

bullets.title: Remote code execution via the B2B ordering endpoint
bullets.body: The B2B order processor evaluates user-supplied order content as executable code inside a sandbox that can be escaped; a crafted order payload gives an attacker full server-side code execution as the application process.

bullets.title: Database dump via the login and search endpoints
bullets.body: Both the login and product-search forms build their database queries by pasting user input directly into the SQL string; an attacker can read every table in the database — including all user credentials — without logging in.

bullets.title: Immediate offline cracking of every stolen password
bullets.body: All user passwords are stored as short, unsalted hashes using an algorithm designed for speed rather than security; any credential stolen via database access, file leak, or key theft can be reversed to plaintext in seconds using freely available tools.

bullets.title: Stored malicious scripts delivered to all admin users
bullets.body: Any attacker who can submit a product review or description can embed a script that executes in every administrator's browser because the admin panel renders stored content as raw HTML without sanitisation and no browser policy blocks inline script execution.

closing: No single patch closes the exposure; the codebase requires parameterised queries, runtime-injected secrets, replacement of the eval-based order processor, and a Content-Security-Policy before any of the above scenarios can be closed.
